selection.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend a few times monthly on social media

Return ONLY a single-line JSON object, with no extra text, no markdown, no comments.

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend a few times monthly on social media

response_parsed.opinion: Look, when I’m out in the yard birdwatching or just catching up on a good novel, I don't want to waste my time wrestling with all this garbage online. Social media, for me, it’s not like it's the main way I get my news; I stick to what I know is solid. But when I do dip in, seeing all this fake stuff, it just gets under my skin. It feels like a waste of good energy. Since I don't spend hours scrolling—I’m not glued to my phone all day like some folks—the battle isn't about constant vigilance; it's about sharp instincts when I do look. The first thing you gotta do is stop reacting immediately. When something wild pops up, especially if it sounds too outrageous or too perfect, you hit the pause button. Don't share it, don't comment on it right away. That’s where the noise gets louder. Instead, you treat it like you’re checking a dodgy engine part—you gotta check the specs. Who made this? Is this a real source, or is it some stranger just trying to stir up trouble? I find that if the post is making me instantly furious or overly excited, that’s usually the biggest red flag. Those strong, instant feelings are often what the fake stuff is designed to trigger. If it’s about politics or some huge drama, I try to see if a couple of reliable local news sources—the ones I actually trust—are saying the same thing. If they aren't, you chalk it up as probably bunk. It’s not about being a computer scientist; it’s about using common sense, the same kind of practical sense I use when I’m diagnosing a sputtering engine. If it doesn't hold up under a little bit of honest scrutiny, you just delete it from your feed. Don't feed the trolls, and don't waste your time arguing with the nonsense. Keep your focus on the things that actually matter, like the next hike or getting a good meal cooked up.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona is highly extraverted (98), low agreeableness (12), low conscientiousness (7), and has a practical, hands-on background (mechanic, lower secondary education). They spend very little time on social media. The tone must be direct, skeptical, energetic, and grounded in practical experience, avoiding overly academic jargon. The response needs to reflect a 'common sense' approach rather than a highly technical one. The advice should be actionable and brief, fitting someone who doesn't want to get bogged down in online drama.
